title: श्री सोमनाथ स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री सोमनाथ स्तोत्रम् ॥

ॐ नमः शिवाय सोमनाथाय।
ज्योतिर्लिङ्गाय प्रभासक्षेत्रवासिने॥

सोमनाथं महादेवं प्रभासक्षेत्रनायकम्।
चन्द्रमौलिं जगन्नाथं प्रणमामि मुहुर्मुहुः॥ १॥

सोमेन स्थापितं लिङ्गं प्रथमं ज्योतिरात्मकम्।
शापमुक्त्यै शशाङ्कस्य सोमनाथं नमाम्यहम्॥ २॥

सागरस्य तटे रम्ये प्रभासे तीर्थमुत्तमम्।
यत्र देवो महादेवः स्वयं ज्योतिर्मयो हरः॥ ३॥

सोमराजेन संपूज्य त्रयस्त्रिंशत्कोटिभिः।
अमरैः सेवितं नित्यं ज्योतिर्लिङ्गं सनातनम्॥ ४॥

स्कन्दोऽपि भगवान् यत्र सिद्धक्षेत्रे व्यवस्थितः।
त्रिवेणीसंगमे पुण्ये सोमनाथो विराजते॥ ५॥

चन्द्रमाः क्षयरोगेण ग्रस्तो दक्षशापतः।
शिवपूजनमात्रेण रोगमुक्तः सुशोभनः॥ ६॥

तत्प्रभावान्महालिङ्गं सोमनाथेति विश्रुतम्।
प्रथमं ज्योतिर्लिङ्गानां वन्दे सोमेश्वरं शिवम्॥ ७॥

त्रिशूलधारिणं देवं नीलकण्ठं पिनाकिनम्।
गजचर्माम्बरधरं सोमनाथं नमाम्यहम्॥ ८॥

सर्वतीर्थमयं क्षेत्रं प्रभासं पुण्यवर्धनम्।
यत्र कृष्णो महायोगी स्वधाम गतवान् प्रभुः॥ ९॥

सोमनाथस्य माहात्म्यं यः पठेत् भक्तिसंयुतः।
सर्वपापविनिर्मुक्तः शिवसायुज्यमाप्नुयात्॥ १०॥

॥ इति श्री सोमनाथ स्तोत्रम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Somnath Stotram ||

Om Namah Shivaya Somnathaya |
Jyotirlingaya Prabhasa-kshetra-vasine ||

Somnatham Mahadevam Prabhasa-kshetra-nayakam |
Chandramaulim Jagannatham pranamami muhur-muhuh || 1 ||

Somena sthapitam lingam prathamam jyotir-atmakam |
Shapa-muktyai shashankasya Somnatham namamyaham || 2 ||

Sagarasya tate ramye Prabhase tirtham-uttamam |
Yatra Devo Mahadevah svayam jyotirmayo Harah || 3 ||

Somarajena sampujya trayastrimshatkotibhih |
Amaraih sevitam nityam Jyotirlingam sanatanam || 4 ||

Skando-api Bhagavan yatra Siddha-kshetre vyavasthitah |
Triveni-sangame punye Somnath virajate || 5 ||

Chandramah kshaya-rogena grasto Daksha-shapatah |
Shiva-pujanam-atrena roga-muktah sushobhanah || 6 ||

Tat-prabhavan-mahalingam Somnatheti vishrutam |
Prathamam Jyotirlinganam vande Someshwaram Shivam || 7 ||

Trishula-dharinam Devam Nilakantham Pinakinam |
Gajacharm-ambara-dharam Somnatham namamyaham || 8 ||

Sarva-tirtha-mayam kshetram Prabhasam punya-vardhanam |
Yatra Krishno Mahayogi svadham gatavan Prabhuh || 9 ||

Somnnathasya mahatmyam yah pathet bhakti-samyutah |
Sarva-papa-vinirmuktah Shiva-sayujyam-apnuyat || 10 ||

|| Iti Shri Somnath Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: प्रभास क्षेत्र के नायक, चन्द्रमौलि, जगन्नाथ सोमनाथ महादेव को बारम्बार प्रणाम। चन्द्रमा (सोम) ने दक्ष के शाप से क्षयरोग ग्रस्त होकर शिव की पूजा की और रोगमुक्त हुए — इसलिए इस लिंग का नाम सोमनाथ पड़ा। यह प्रथम ज्योतिर्लिंग है, सागर के रम्य तट पर प्रभास तीर्थ में स्वयं ज्योतिर्मय हर विराजमान हैं। तैंतीस करोड़ देवताओं द्वारा सेवित, सनातन ज्योतिर्लिंग है। यहीं भगवान कृष्ण ने महाप्रयाण किया। इस स्तोत्र के पाठ से सर्व पाप नष्ट होते हैं और शिव सायुज्य प्राप्त होता है।
benefits: Equivalent to pilgrimage to Somnath, the first Jyotirlinga
Traditionally recited for removal of all sins accumulated over lifetimes
Invokes liberation and union with lord shiva
Traditionally recited for removal of the effects of curses and planetary afflictions
Associated in tradition with chronic illnesses as soma (moon) was healed
Invokes the blessing of prosperity and divine protection
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shiva, Somnath Stotram (श्री सोमनाथ स्तोत्रम्) stands as a devotional hymn of praise. This sacred composition is sourced from Shiva Purana and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Regular recitation is believed to destroy all sins accumulated over lifetimes, grant liberation and union with Lord Shiva, and equivalent to pilgrimage to Somnath, the first Jyotirlinga. This prayer is traditionally recited on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar to invoke divine grace. With 10 verses and a reading time of about 7 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Somnath Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Somnath Stotram in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. Sacred hymn to first Jyotirlinga at Prabhasa Kshetra, Gujarat.
relatedSearches: Somnath temple Gujarat Prabhasa kshetra history
Someshwara moon god Daksha curse story
Chandra Shiva moon waxing waning legend

seoFaqs.question: What is the textual relationship between the Somnath Stotram and the Dwadasha Jyotirlinga Stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: The Somnath Stotram is most commonly the opening section of the Dwadasha Jyotirlinga Stotra, which begins with 'Saurashtre Somanatham cha Shrishaile Mallikarjunam' (At Saurashtra is Somanatha, at Shrishailam is Mallikarjuna). This verse names the first two of the twelve Jyotirlingas. As a standalone 'Somnath Stotram' devotees usually recite either this opening verse, an extended meditation on Someshwara as the moon-bearing Shiva of Prabhasa Kshetra, or a curated collection of Skanda Purana verses describing the Somnath origin story. Greenmesg, Hindupedia and Vedadhara reproduce the Dwadasha Jyotirlinga Stotra with the Somnath verse intact. The Somnath verse is therefore embedded within a larger pan-Indian Shiva pilgrimage hymn rather than being an independent composition.

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning and origin of the name 'Somnath'?
seoFaqs.answer: 'Somnath' literally means 'Lord of Soma', where Soma is the moon god (and also the Vedic ritual plant whose juice was offered in yajna). The Skanda Purana and the Linga Purana preserve the origin story: Daksha cursed his son-in-law Soma (the moon) to wane into nothingness as punishment for favouring only one of his 27 wives (the nakshatras, all Daksha's daughters). Soma performed severe penance to Shiva at Prabhasa Kshetra on the Saurashtra coast. Shiva, pleased, granted Soma partial relief, allowing him to wax and wane cyclically rather than disappear forever. Shiva then took the name Someshwara (Lord of Soma) and remained eternally at this site as the Jyotirlinga. The Somnath shrine in Gujarat enshrines this manifestation, and the stotram celebrates this moon-redeeming aspect of Shiva.

seoFaqs.question: Why is Somnath listed first among the twelve Jyotirlingas?
seoFaqs.answer: The Skanda Purana and the standard Dwadasha Jyotirlinga enumeration begin with Somnath because of its primacy in three respects. First, chronological primacy: the Soma legend places this Jyotirlinga manifestation at the dawn of the cosmic age. Second, geographic primacy: Saurashtra (modern Gujarat) was a central pilgrimage region in the classical period, and Prabhasa Kshetra is mentioned in the Mahabharata as one of the most sacred coastal tirthas. Third, narrative primacy: the moon-curse story is one of the most accessible and widely recited Puranic episodes, making Somnath the natural starting point. The opening verse 'Saurashtre Somanatham cha' has therefore become the standard incipit of the entire Jyotirlinga recitation tradition.

seoFaqs.question: What historical significance does the Somnath temple carry?
seoFaqs.answer: The Somnath temple has been destroyed and rebuilt at least seven times across recorded history, including the famous raid by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1026 CE and subsequent invasions. The current temple was rebuilt in the Maru-Gurjara architectural style and inaugurated in 1951 by Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel and President Rajendra Prasad after India's independence, becoming an iconic symbol of cultural restoration. The Somnath Jyotirlinga is therefore unique among the twelve in carrying both deep mythological and significant modern political-cultural meaning. Devotees who recite the Somnath Stotram or the wider Dwadasha Jyotirlinga Stotra are participating in a chant whose specific shrine references span more than a millennium of pilgrimage history, even though the verses themselves are textually Puranic in origin.

seoFaqs.question: When and how is the Somnath / Dwadasha Jyotirlinga Stotram traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The recitation is part of daily nitya-paath in Shaiva households, especially recommended in the morning after bath as a substitute pilgrimage. It is intensified on Mondays, Pradosham evenings, Maha Shivaratri, Shravan Mondays and Karthika Mondays. The phala-shruti embedded in the stotra promises that one who recites the names of the twelve Jyotirlingas morning and evening daily is freed from the sins of seven lifetimes, and the recitation is treated as karmically equivalent to physical pilgrimage to all twelve sites. The standard posture is seated facing east or north before a Shiva image or lingam, with vibhuti applied. Total recitation of the stotra takes 4 to 6 minutes at moderate tempo, fitting comfortably into any daily puja sequence.

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the closing phala-shruti specifically promise?
seoFaqs.answer: The phala-shruti verses of the Dwadasha Jyotirlinga Stotra explicitly state that whoever recites this stotra in the morning and evening with devotion gains liberation from the accumulated sins of seven previous births (sapta-janma-kritam papam smaranam pranasam smaranam). Devshoppe, Hindupedia and Greenmesg translations preserve this phala-shruti consistently. The recitation also bestows the merit of having visited each of the twelve Jyotirlinga shrines physically, which is significant for devotees unable to undertake the long pan-Indian pilgrimage circuit covering Saurashtra, Andhra Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Maharashtra, Varanasi, Maharashtra (Trimbakeshwar), Jharkhand, Maharashtra (Nashik area), Tamil Nadu and Maharashtra. The stotra thus functions as both a devotional recitation and a vicarious pilgrimage practice.
